english: pa (pa) and ˳brúm-pa nág-po ('brum pa nag po) as name of a disease of the groin, prob. bubo Med. — ˳brum-rjes ('brum rjes) pock-mark. — ˳brúm-po ('brum po) a large grain Cs.; ˳brúm-bu ('brum bu) a small grain; pock, pustule, v. above.
Sch. a tutelar goddess of little children, worshipped by the Shamans.

pf. and imp. bres (bres) to draw over or before, to spread, to stretch, a net Glr., a curtain Glr., a canopy, awning Lex.; to wrap a thing up in a cloth, in order to carry it, as books, a corpse Thgy.

pf. breg(s) (bregs), imp. brog(s) (brogs), also ˳drég-pa ('dreg pa) to cut off, šiṅ-ta-lai lo-ma bregs-pa (shing ta la'i lo ma bregs pa) a plantain branch cut off, as representing a being irremediably cut off from its former state of existence Mil.; to mow Sch.; of parts of the body: ske (ske) to cut off a person's neck Thgr., p´o-mtsán (pho mtshan) the membrum virile Schr., rtai súg-pa (rta'i sug pa) the foot of a horse, prob. only the tendon of it, as much as to lame, to disable Glr.; also to sever with a saw; most frq. in reference to the hair, to cut off, to shave, with the scissors or a razor, skra daṅ k´á-spu (skra dang kha spu) frq.; ˳brég-mkhan ('breg mkhan) barber, hair-cutter Dzl.; ˳breg-spyád ('breg spyad) a sharp small knife Sch.

Cs., bréṅ-ba (breng ba) strap, rope, ko-˳bréṅ (ko 'breng) leather strap; ša-˳breṅ (sha 'breng) Mil.; ˳breṅ-tag ('breng thag) Cs. cane-ribbon, made of buck-leather; leading-rope, guide-line. — ˳bréṅ-bu ('breng bu) Cs. cobbler's strap.

connection, conjunction, yet only in certain applications: 1. connection between cause and effect, used also at once for effect, consequence, efficacy, smón-lam-gyi ˳brél-pa (smon lam gyi 'brel pa) the efficacy of prayer Mil. frq.; ˳jog-pa ('jog pa) to apply, make use of it Mil. — 2. the vascular and nervous system conjunctively, the two systems in their totality, ni (ni) f., Med. — 3. genitive case, the sixth case of Tibetan Grammarians, ˳brél-pai sgra ('brel pa'i sgra), the termination of it, kyi (kyi). — 4. a small quantity, a little, a bit, zás-kyi ˳brél-pa žig dgos (zas kyi 'brel pa zhig dgos) I ask for a little bit to eat Mil. frq.; čos(-kyi) ˳brél(-pa) tób-pa (chos (kyi) 'brel (pa) thob pa) to snatch up a little bit of religion Mil.

I. vb., intrs. of sbrél-ba (sbrel ba), 1. to hang together, to cohere, to be connected, rtsa daṅ rus-pa tsam ˳brél-ba (rtsa dang rus pa tsam 'brel ba) connected only by veins and bones, nothing but skin and bone Dzl.; ˳od-zér-gyi drá-bas ˳brél-te ('od zer gyi dra bas 'brel te) covered with a continuous net of rays Glr.; gen. with daṅ (dang), bod daṅ rgyai ˳brél-tsul (bod dang rgya'i 'brel tshul) the connection with, or the intercourse between Tibet and China Glr.; de daṅ ˳brél-bai las (de dang 'brel ba'i las) the functions connected with, and peculiar to (a certain organ) Lt.; ˳brel-mtsams ('brel mtshams) 1. joint, or rivet of pincers etc. S.g. 2. boundary, W. — 2. to come together, to meet, to join, ˳brél-gtam ('brel gtam) gossipings in meeting on the road Mil. — 3. to meet sexually, to cohabit, de daṅ lus ˳brél-ba (de dang lus 'brel ba) to cohabit with (him or her) Glr.; (lhán-du) ˳brél-ba-la(s)bu skyes ((lhan du) 'brel ba la(s) bu skyes) they having cohabited, a child was born Glr. — II. sbst. ˳brél-ba ('brel ba) or ˳brel ('brel) union, communication, connection, bod daṅ ˳brel byuṅ (bod dang 'brel byung) the union with Tibet took place Glr.; rgya bod gnyis ˳brel čád (rgya bod gnyis 'brel chad) the union ceases Glr.; *nor-ḍél čó-če, nor-ḍél-la čá-če* (*nor drel co ce, nor drel la ca ce*) W., to form a mercantile connection, to enter into commercial intercourse. — las-˳brel (las 'brel) = las-˳p´ro (las 'phro) q.v. — gnyis-˳brél (gnyis 'brel), gsum-˳brél (gsum 'brel) a double, triple consonant, e.g. sk (sk), skr (skr).

solitude, wilderness, uncultivated land, esp. summer-pasture for cattle in the mountains; thus ˳brog-skyoṅ-ba ('brog skyong ba) Ld.-Glr., Schl. 15, 6 might imply: to attend to a mountain dairy; gám-˳brog (gam 'brog) a near, rgyáṅ-˳brog (rgyang 'brog) a remote summer-pasture; ˳brog-kyi ('brog kyi) Cs. a large shaggy shepherd's dog; ˳bróg-dgon ('brog dgon), ˳bróg-stoṅ ('brog stong), ˳bróg-sa ('brog sa) = ˳brog ('brog). ˳bróg-gnas ('brog gnas) 1. pasture-land 2. people occupying it. — ˳bróg-pa ('brog pa), ˳bróg-mi ('brog mi) id.; more particularly, inhabitants of the steppe, nomadic Tibetans Sch., ˳bróg-mo ('brog mo) wife, ˳brog-p´rug ('brog phrug) child of such a nomad. — ˳brog-žád ('brog zhad) Sch. rude, rough, boorish, ˳brog-žad stón-pa ('brog zhad ston pa) to be rude etc.
1. = gyag-rgod (g.yag rgod), wild yak Glr.; byáṅ-kai ˳broṅ (byang ka'i 'brong), the yak of
